labels.useWeatherTooltip: Account for weather when calculating passes using weather forecasts

help.overview: The Sensor Timeline shows when a network of sensors can see the selected satellite over the coming hours. Each row is a sensor and each colored bar is a pass, so you can compare every site's opportunities at a glance and pick the best sensor for the next observation.
help.imgAlt: The Sensor Timeline showing color coded passes for several sensors
help.imgCaption: Each row is a sensor; bar color shows the quality of each pass of the selected satellite.
help.reading:
• Green: a long pass, above the average pass threshold.
  • Yellow: an average pass.
  • Red: a short pass, below the bad pass threshold.
  • Light gray row: that sensor never sees the satellite in the window.
Hover a bar for the exact start and end times. With Detailed Plot enabled, optical sensors gain extra rows showing the raw conditions: In Field of View, Satellite in Sunlight, Station in Eclipse, and Clear Skies; a pass is observable only where all of them line up.
help.settings: The settings panel controls the calculation: Calculation Length (Hours) sets the window, Calculation Interval (Seconds) trades speed for precision, and the Bad Pass Length and Average Pass Length thresholds define the red, yellow, and green coloring. Use Weather folds hourly cloud cover forecasts into optical sensor passes. The buttons at the bottom toggle individual sensors: red means excluded from the calculation.
help.howToUse:
1. Select a satellite; the bottom icon stays disabled until one is selected.
  2. Open the Sensor Timeline from the bottom menu. The timeline computes and draws automatically.
  3. Open the settings panel to change the window, thresholds, plot detail, weather use, or which sensors are included; the plot recalculates when you change a value.
  4. Click a pass to switch to that sensor and jump the clock to the pass start, ready for look angles or observation planning.
  5. Use the download button to export the pass list as a CSV file.
help.tip1: Weather comes from the Open-Meteo cloud cover forecast for each optical site; radars ignore weather, sunlight, and darkness conditions entirely.
help.tip2: Clicking a pass re-selects the satellite after changing sensor and time, so other open menus refresh with the new geometry.
help.tip3: Shorter calculation intervals catch brief passes but take longer to compute; 30 seconds is a good balance for a 24 hour window.
